_subsystem_plan.subsystem_role: External cable carrier from base to wrist, drag chain or spiral, signal + power + pneumatic
physics_paths: Load path: Cable spine supports weight and tension of cables from base to wrist
Flow path: Signal, power, and pneumatic cables routed through spine
Thermal path: Heat dissipation from cables and spine to ambient air
integration_opportunities: Replaceable module path: Cable spine remains removable without full teardown
Cable harness visibility: Harnesses are routed through visible raceways or protected channels
Access not just text: Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover
Harness strain relief and bend radius: Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius
Connector accessibility and keying: Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating
Axis datum readability: J1-J6 axis rings, reducer/bearing dummies, encoder covers, and tool flange datum are visible
Cable spine serviceability: Cable spine clips externally or remains inspectable through open channels
Joint cartridge replaceability: Joint cartridges can be removed without cutting printed links
Bearing shaft mounting sanity: Each joint shows a bearing seat with shoulder or retaining feature, shaft locating geometry, and stated fit intent for inner/outer races
Moving joint harness workmanship: Harness crossing each joint has slack loop, strain relief at both ends, and routing that avoids pinch points through the full motion range
Connection type named: Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type with its DOF and a numeric clearance
Assembly sequence feasible: Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable
Datum chain continuity: One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme
Load path closure: Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types

Structural development direction: The design moves the right direction: fewer interfaces, parts consolidated where service is preserved, open load paths closed, and redundancy added only at critical joints
Interface minimization: The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer
verification_focus: Load path closure: Ensure every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground
Assembly sequence feasibility: Ensure parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable
